original_hypothesis.title: Predictable On-Chain Randomness in NextGenRandomizerNXT Allows Trait Sniping
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: NextGenRandomizerNXT.calculateTokenHash uses keccak256(abi.encodePacked(_mintIndex, blockhash(block.number - 1), randoms.randomNumber(), randoms.randomWord())). Both randomNumber() and randomWord() in XRandoms use block.prevrandao, blockhash(block.number-1), and block.timestamp - all known to the caller at execution time. A minter using a smart contract can predict the exact token hash before committing to the mint, selectively minting only when rare traits would be generated. The _saltfun_o parameter is unused in the hash computation, providing no additional entropy.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: All inputs to the hash function are deterministic at execution time: mintIndex is calculable from viewCirSupply, block variables are known to the executing contract, and XRandoms provides no independent entropy. This enables selective minting for trait manipulation.

proof_of_signal.pattern: hash = keccak256(abi.encodePacked(_mintIndex, blockhash(block.number-1), randoms.randomNumber(), randoms.randomWord())); all inputs deterministic at execution time

preconditions: Collection uses NextGenRandomizerNXT as its randomizer (not VRF or ARRNG)
Token traits/rarity derived from tokenToHash are economically valuable
Attacker deploys contract that computes hash before committing to mint

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: All hash inputs are block-level variables known to any executing contract; no commit-reveal scheme; _saltfun_o unused
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Any mint on collections using NextGenRandomizerNXT

operational_questions: Which collections use NextGenRandomizerNXT vs VRF/ARRNG?
How are token traits derived from tokenToHash?
What is the economic value differential between common and rare traits?
validation_requirements: Deploy contract that pre-computes hash and selectively mints
Verify hash prediction matches actual token hash
Test with actual generative art scripts to confirm trait selection

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Deploy contract that computes the would-be token hash using current block variables
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: Contract checks if predicted hash produces desirable traits
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: If desirable, proceed with mint; if not, revert to save gas (minus base cost)
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Repeat across blocks until rare traits are captured

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Rare traits systematically captured by attackers; fair distribution destroyed
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Token hashes immutable once set; cannot retroactively fix trait distribution

execution_requirements_analysis.technical_skills: Moderate - needs understanding of hash-to-trait mapping
execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Any active mint period for NXT-randomized collections

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Contract-based minters with high rare-trait frequency
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Statistical anomalies in trait distribution by minter type
investigation_priorities.behavioral_patterns: Minting contracts that frequently revert then succeed

validation_result.impact_summary: Attackers can predict token hashes before minting, allowing them to selectively mint only rare NFTs and revert otherwise, destroying fair distribution.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The `NextGenRandomizerNXT` contract generates randomness synchronously during the minting process. When `calculateTokenHash` is called, it computes a hash using `_mintIndex`, `blockhash(block.number - 1)`, and outputs from the `XRandoms` contract (`randomNumber()` and `randomWord()`). The `XRandoms` contract generates these outputs using `block.prevrandao`, `blockhash(block.number - 1)`, and `block.timestamp`. Because all of these variables are deterministic and known to any smart contract executing in the same block, an attacker can write a smart contract that pre-computes the exact `tokenHash` that will be assigned to the next minted token. The attacker's contract can then evaluate whether this hash corresponds to rare or valuable traits. If the traits are desirable, the contract proceeds with the call to `NextGenMinterContract.mint()`; if not, it simply skips the mint or reverts the transaction to save the minting cost. This completely bypasses the randomness, allowing trait sniping. Furthermore, the `_saltfun_o` parameter, which could have been used to inject user-provided entropy or a commit-reveal salt, is entirely ignored in `calculateTokenHash`.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The evidence clearly shows that `calculateTokenHash` in `RandomizerNXT.sol` relies solely on block variables and the predictable `_mintIndex`. The `XRandoms.sol` contract also relies purely on block variables. There is no commit-reveal scheme or external oracle used in this specific randomizer, making the randomness 100% predictable to a calling smart contract.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The hash is computed synchronously using block variables and predictable external calls, ignoring the `_saltfun_o` parameter. This allows a calling contract to pre-compute the exact hash.
Relies entirely on block variables (`prevrandao`, `blockhash`, `timestamp`) which are identical for all transactions within the same block and readable by the attacker's contract.
Also relies entirely on the same block variables, providing no additional entropy and remaining completely predictable.

execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: When a user calls `mint()` on the minter contract, it calculates the `mintIndex` and calls `gencore.mint()`. The core contract increments the supply and calls `_mintProcessing()`, which synchronously calls `randomizer.calculateTokenHash()`. In `NextGenRandomizerNXT`, the hash is immediately calculated using block variables and set on the core contract via `setTokenHash()`. Because the entire chain of calls happens synchronously in one transaction, an attacker's contract can simulate the exact same logic before invoking `mint()`.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: The fixturesDeployment.js script explicitly deploys NextGenRandomizerNXT and randomPool, demonstrating that this randomizer is an intended option for collections.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: An attacker deploys a smart contract with a function `snipeMint(uint256 colId)`. This function reads `gencore.viewCirSupply(colId)` to determine the upcoming `mintIndex`. It then computes the expected hash using `keccak256(abi.encodePacked(mintIndex, blockhash(block.number - 1), randoms.randomNumber(), randoms.randomWord()))`. The contract checks if this hash maps to a rare trait. If it does, it calls `minter.mint{value: price}(...)`. If it does not, the transaction simply completes without minting (or reverts), costing the attacker only base gas fees. The attacker runs this continuously until they capture the rarest NFTs.
validation_result.impact_assessment: High. The core value proposition of generative art and randomized NFT collections relies on fair, unpredictable distribution. If attackers can snipe all rare traits, regular users are left with only common traits, destroying the collection's economic fairness and reputation.
recommended_mitigations: Do not use synchronous block variables for randomness in NFT minting.
Use a commit-reveal scheme where the user commits to a mint, and the randomness is fulfilled in a subsequent block.

Deprecate `NextGenRandomizerNXT` for any collection where trait rarity has economic value, and enforce the use of `NextGenRandomizerVRF` or `NextGenRandomizerRNG` which use asynchronous oracle callbacks.
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy NextGenCore, MinterContract, XRandoms, and NextGenRandomizerNXT. 2. Create a collection and configure it to use NextGenRandomizerNXT. 3. Deploy an AttackerContract that calculates the hash using the exact logic from RandomizerNXT. 4. Have the AttackerContract call `mint()` only if the first byte of the predicted hash is `0x00` (simulating a rare trait). 5. Verify that all tokens minted by the AttackerContract have hashes starting with `0x00`.
